summary:
The Battle of Sekigahara (October 21, 1600 AD) was the decisive engagement that ended Japan's century of civil war and established Tokugawa Ieyasu's supremacy, directly leading to the foundation of the [[event:2336|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and 265 years of unified Japanese rule under one family. The battle was the culmination of a power struggle following the death of [[event:3237|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] in 1598: Ieyasu had been Hideyoshi's most powerful vassal and had been named one of five regents for Hideyoshi's infant son Toyotomi Hideyori. The regency broke down as daimyo (feudal lords) aligned into two coalitions — the Eastern Army (pro-Tokugawa) and the Western Army (ostensibly defending the Toyotomi succession, led by Ishida Mitsunari).

Approximately 160,000 warriors gathered at the mountain pass of Sekigahara on a foggy October morning. The battle's outcome was decided by treachery: Kobayakawa Hideaki, a western daimyo whose prior negotiations with Ieyasu had been kept secret, dramatically changed sides mid-battle, attacking the western flank and triggering a general rout. The Western Army was destroyed in six hours; Ishida Mitsunari was captured and beheaded. Ieyasu systematically redistributed confiscated lands, punishing enemy daimyo and rewarding allies — creating the geographic pattern of fudai (inner lords, traditionally allied with Tokugawa) and tozama (outer lords, former enemies) that would define Tokugawa political geography and the fault lines that eventually produced the Meiji Restoration. In 1603, Ieyasu received the title of Shogun from the Emperor, establishing a hereditary line that confirmed the Tokugawa's exclusive right to the shogunate.
